text:
# Abstract

Generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) technology has rapidly evolved and there is growing concern of how AI-generated content can be misused within academic settings. The developments of the detection tools have ignited a race, with the aim of creating and developing an adequate mechanism. Our study assesses and discusses the validity, reliability and accuracy of the academic detection tools created in response to the “GPT crisis” and aims to investigate the validity of a model to accurately discern whether the text produced has been generated by a human or by ChatGPT and explore whether there is a considerable reduction in accuracy with modifications to the text, such as machine translation or content obscuration.

We tested 14 tools, including 12 publicly available detectors as well as two commercial plagiarism checkers (Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck). None of the tools we tested provided reliable or accurate results. All but one of the publicly available detectors we tested (and all of the commercial tools) were heavily biased towards human writers, meaning they regularly falsely labelled text generated by AI as having been written by a human. The performance of all the tools degraded dramatically when obfuscation techniques were used.

This paper makes the following contributions: (1) It provides an in-depth survey of the state of the art in AI detection research and practice from both scientific and non-scientific perspectives; (2) It provides one of the most thorough and carefully-designed comparative evaluation efforts to date, using a novel test set of documents; and (3) It reflects on some of the implications of using such tools for enforcing academic integrity. We conclude that today's detection tools are not reliable enough for use in high-stakes academic decision-making.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
